title: Nvidia Uses The Specter of Huawei to Make Its Chip Exports Case - The Wire China
gist: Nvidia lobbied Washington for H200 export relief to China by warning that Huawei and SMIC could soon 'satisfy global AI chip demand,' but independent analysts (CFR's Chris McGuire, researcher Lennart Heim, Epoch AI) say Nvidia's own report actually understates its lead — Huawei's real output is a low single-digit percent of Nvidia's, its chips lag on memory and performance, and its TSMC-chip stockpile via illegal diversion shows export controls are constraining it, not failing.

insights.claim: Nvidia's internal report to the U.S. government warned that Huawei's chip stockpile and growing SMIC manufacturing capacity give it 'the flexibility to satisfy global AI chip demand,' and that 'if Nvidia is kept out of the China market, Huawei production and sales will soar' — without specifying a timeframe or comparing Nvidia and Huawei chip performance.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Identifies the exact lobbying mechanism Nvidia used to win policy relief — investors should expect Nvidia to redeploy this 'Huawei threat' framing whenever it seeks further export-control easing.
insights.quote: If Nvidia is kept out of the China market, Huawei production and sales will soar
insights.locator: In a report that Nvidia presented to the U.S. government

insights.claim: The Nvidia pitch combined with Jensen Huang's personal diplomacy succeeded: President Trump announced in December 2025 that Nvidia could sell its H200 chip (launched in 2023) to China, reversing Biden-era export controls under which Nvidia's China market share had fallen to zero.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Zero is the revenue baseline the H200 approval is meant to reverse — a concrete reference point for modeling any China revenue recovery for Nvidia.
insights.quote: Nvidia’s market share in China has fallen to zero
insights.locator: Since the Biden administration first imposed export controls

insights.claim: Nvidia's report estimated Huawei sold 200,000–450,000 AI chips in 2024 and would sell 1 million in 2025, with SMIC set to produce over 700,000 of Huawei's advanced Ascend 910C chips by end of this year (50,000/month) and several million of the less-advanced Ascend 910B by 2027.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: These are the headline figures driving the export-control debate; because they originate from an interested party (Nvidia) rather than independent verification, they should be treated as an upper-bound lobbying estimate, not a market forecast.
insights.locator: Nvidia estimated that Huawei sold between 200,000 and 450,000

insights.claim: Nvidia's own estimate of up to 1 million Huawei chip sales in 2025 far exceeds Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's congressional testimony last June that China could produce only about 200,000 advanced chips in 2025 — a five-fold gap between Nvidia's lobbying numbers and the administration's own official estimate.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The internal inconsistency between Nvidia's figures and the Commerce Department's own testimony is a credibility gap that could resurface and complicate Nvidia's case in future export-control fights.
insights.locator: Those numbers far exceed Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick’s estimate

themes.theme: Independent benchmarks show the gap is still huge

insights.claim: CFR senior fellow Chris McGuire estimates that even 800,000 Ascend 910C chips — the high end of production estimates for 2025 — would amount to 'just 5.3 percent of the total processing power' of Nvidia's 2025 chip production, and that Nvidia's performance lead will keep widening even if Huawei reaches 2 million chips in 2026 and 4 million in 2027.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A credible third-party benchmark investors can use to size the real competitive threat from Huawei, versus Nvidia's self-interested framing in its own lobbying report.
insights.quote: just 5.3 percent of the total processing power
insights.locator: In a report published in December, Chris McGuire

insights.claim: Chris McGuire, a former U.S. national-security official who helped design chip export controls, states that Huawei 'simply does not pose a competitive threat to Nvidia globally right now' because its chips are far behind on quality and it cannot manufacture them at sufficient volume.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts the premise underlying Nvidia's export-relief lobbying — a bear case for any 'Huawei is catching up' narrative used to justify further loosening controls.
insights.quote: Huawei simply does not pose a competitive threat to Nvidia globally right now.
insights.locator: “Huawei simply does not pose a competitive threat

insights.claim: Independent research firm Epoch AI found Huawei made just 6% of all AI chips sold worldwide in Q1 2026, falling to roughly half that share once adjusted for the lower computational power of its chips relative to Nvidia's.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: A hard, third-party global market-share number for anyone modeling China's AI-chip self-sufficiency trajectory — well below the impression created by Nvidia's own stockpile-and-capacity figures.
insights.quote: Huawei made just 6 percent of the total AI chips sold worldwide during the first quarter of this year, and half that amount after adjusting
insights.locator: Huawei made just 6 percent of the total AI chips

insights.claim: McGuire notes the irony that Nvidia's performance lead over Huawei stems substantially from U.S. export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment, which have degraded China's own AI-chip production capability.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Suggests equipment-level export controls are working as designed even as chip-sale controls (like the H200 approval) get relaxed — an important nuance when evaluating whether easing controls undermines the policy's own logic.
insights.locator: Ironically, he adds, Nvidia has a lead over Huawei

themes.theme: Export controls are working, not failing

insights.claim: Nvidia's own report cited Reuters reporting that Huawei stockpiled 2–3 million AI chips manufactured by TSMC and obtained via illegal diversion through Chinese company Sophgo; researcher Lennart Heim argues this actually proves export controls are constraining Huawei, since it wouldn't need TSMC-made chips if it could produce enough itself.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Reframes Nvidia's own cited evidence as proof export controls are limiting Huawei's domestic output, undercutting the case Nvidia is making for further easing.
insights.quote: If they were able to produce that many chips themselves, why would they go to TSMC?
insights.locator: “If they were able to produce that many chips

insights.claim: Lennart Heim says the 2–3 million TSMC-diverted chips in Huawei's stockpile are 'tiny' next to the more than 60 million 910C-equivalent chips the U.S. will have produced between January 2025 and end of 2026.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Puts the US-China compute gap into one comparable unit (910C-equivalents), giving investors a concrete scale reference for how far behind Huawei's stockpile really is.
insights.locator: Even though figures such as 2 to 3 million AI chips sound significant

insights.claim: Huawei's newest chips — the 950PR launched in March 2026 and another chip due later this year — still have slower memory than Nvidia's H200 and lag even further behind Nvidia's next-generation Blackwell chips, according to Lennart Heim.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Even Huawei's latest hardware fails to close the performance gap, tempering any thesis of imminent Huawei technical parity with Nvidia's product line.
insights.locator: Huawei has certainly been trying to improve its offering

insights.claim: Export-control expert Gregory Allen says Nvidia is trying to 'scare U.S. policymakers that Huawei is this great big threat,' arguing Huawei is a threat, 'but not in the way Nvidia is claiming,' since raw chip volume is not equivalent to competitiveness.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Names the specific rhetorical tactic (volume-as-threat) that investors should discount when evaluating Nvidia's future export-policy claims.
insights.quote: scare U.S. policymakers that Huawei is this great big threat
insights.locator: Nvidia is attempting to “scare U.S. policymakers

insights.claim: Gregory Allen says Nvidia has damaged its own credibility on Capitol Hill by presenting numbers that were 'incredibly difficult to believe,' and hopes this gives Congress, staffers, and the White House pause before taking Nvidia's figures at face value in the future.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: Signals rising skepticism in Washington toward Nvidia's lobbying claims — a political risk factor for Nvidia's ability to win further export relief going forward.
insights.quote: Nvidia has really damaged their credibility on Capitol Hill by saying things that were incredibly difficult to believe.

insights.claim: Despite the Trump administration clearing H200 exports, the Chinese government has itself locked Nvidia out of the domestic China market as of this report — meaning Nvidia's Washington lobbying win has not yet translated into actual China sales.
insights.speaker: Ella Apostoaie
insights.so_what: The U.S. policy win is not revenue: investors should not assume H200 approval alone restores near-term China sales given Beijing-side restrictions.
insights.locator: The Chinese government has to date locked Nvidia out
